skill.name: latex-posters
skill.description: Create conference-ready research posters with LaTeX templates, layout guidance, typography rules, and PDF quality checks.
skill.summary: Build academic research posters in LaTeX with templates, design guidance, and print-readiness checks.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows the weak-cryptography and most external-command hits are LaTeX or Markdown false positives. The confirmed risk is a legitimate Bash PDF review script that invokes local PDF utilities on a user-supplied file; no malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or hidden network behavior was found. Publish with a warning to run command-line checks only on trusted files.

medium_findings.title: Local PDF Review Script Uses External Commands
medium_findings.description: The repository includes a Bash script that runs local tools such as pdfinfo, pdffonts, pdfimages, ls, grep, awk, head, tail, wc, and tr against a user-supplied PDF path. The file path is quoted and the commands are read-only, so command injection was not found, but parsing untrusted PDFs with local utilities is still an operational risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell script clearly invokes external PDF inspection utilities on a user-provided file. The commands are hardcoded and arguments are quoted, which lowers confidence that this is exploitable or malicious.

low_findings.title: Shell Examples In Documentation
low_findings.description: Many external-command detections are Markdown examples for compiling LaTeX, checking logs, and inspecting generated PDFs. These examples are relevant to the skill and do not execute automatically, but users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation or fenced code examples rather than hidden execution paths. The risk is user-mediated command use, not automatic execution.

low_findings.title: Placeholder URLs In Poster Templates
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings are DOI, GitHub, and design-tool links used for QR codes or reference resources. They are visible template content and not evidence of network calls, tracking, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in LaTeX QR-code examples and reference lists. No code path was found that fetches these URLs or sends local data to them.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are LaTeX False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography detections resolve to LaTeX font commands, poster section text, statistics notation, Markdown headings, package names, and generated audit text. No cryptographic implementation or weak hash usage was found in the reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines contain LaTeX or Markdown content, not crypto APIs. The pattern class is not semantically applicable to this poster-template skill.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Detections Are Defensive Checks Or Poster Terms
low_findings.description: System and network reconnaissance detections are command-availability checks, PDF inspection commands, or ordinary poster-design text. No evidence was found of host inventory collection, port scanning, credential discovery, or outbound probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script check uses command -v before running optional PDF tools, and other examples are normal documentation content. I did not find semantic evidence of reconnaissance intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local PDF Review Script Uses External Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The repository includes a Bash script that runs local tools such as pdfinfo, pdffonts, pdfimages, ls, grep, awk, head, tail, wc, and tr against a user-supplied PDF path. The file path is quoted and the commands are read-only, so command injection was not found, but parsing untrusted PDFs with local utilities is still an operational risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell script clearly invokes external PDF inspection utilities on a user-provided file. The commands are hardcoded and arguments are quoted, which lowers confidence that this is exploitable or malicious.

content.value_statement: Academic posters require readable layouts, correct print sizes, and clear visual hierarchy. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce LaTeX poster drafts, templates, and review checklists.
actual_capabilities: Selects between beamerposter, tikzposter, and baposter based on poster style and layout needs.
Provides ready-to-customize LaTeX poster templates for common academic sections.
Guides poster typography, color contrast, visual hierarchy, and white space decisions.
Helps convert research paper content into concise poster sections and figure captions.
Includes a PDF review workflow for page size, font embedding, image listing, and manual checks.
Documents standard poster sizes, QR code placement, references, and print-readiness practices.
limitations: It does not guarantee that every generated LaTeX file compiles without local package installation.
It cannot verify final print colors or physical readability without a test print.
Several referenced helper scripts are described in documentation but are not present in this package.
The included review script depends on local PDF tools such as poppler utilities.

use_cases.title: Prepare a conference poster
use_cases.description: Turn research aims, methods, results, and conclusions into a structured LaTeX poster draft.

use_cases.title: Create a lab poster template
use_cases.description: Build a reusable poster template with consistent branding, section layout, and figure placement.

use_cases.title: Review poster print readiness
use_cases.description: Check dimensions, font embedding, image quality, QR codes, and visual readability before submission.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a poster package
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between beamerposter, tikzposter, and baposter for a research poster. My conference size is A0 portrait, and I need three columns with four figures.

prompt_templates.title: Draft poster content
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert paper notes into poster sections
prompt_templates.prompt: Create concise poster text from this research summary. Include title, objective, methods, three result blocks, conclusions, references, and figure caption suggestions.

prompt_templates.title: Customize a template
prompt_templates.prompt: Modify a LaTeX poster template for a 36 by 48 inch landscape poster. Use a high-contrast institutional color scheme and reserve about half the area for figures.

prompt_templates.title: Audit final poster quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my poster plan for print readiness. Check layout balance, font sizes, figure resolution, QR code placement, color contrast, file size, and likely LaTeX compilation issues.

output_examples.input: I need an A0 conference poster for a biology study with three main findings.
output_examples.output: A structured poster plan with a title area, three-column layout, methods block, three result panels, conclusion block, QR code area, and recommended figure sizes.

output_examples.input: My poster has too much text and small figures.
output_examples.output: A revision plan that reduces body text, converts paragraphs into bullets, enlarges result figures, and improves section hierarchy for distance reading.

output_examples.input: Check whether my PDF is ready for submission.
output_examples.output: A practical checklist covering page dimensions, single-page output, embedded fonts, image quality, file size, accessibility, and test-print review.

best_practices: Confirm conference dimensions and orientation before choosing a template.
Keep body text large, concise, and organized around visual results.
Run PDF checks and review a reduced-scale test print before final submission.
anti_patterns: Do not fill the poster with dense paragraphs or small tables.
Do not leave placeholder logos, DOI links, QR codes, or author details unchanged.
Do not run the PDF review script on untrusted files without local security precautions.

faq.question: Which LaTeX poster packages does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers beamerposter, tikzposter, and baposter with templates and package-specific guidance.

faq.question: Can it create a complete poster from my paper?
faq.answer: It can draft the poster structure and text, but you must verify scientific accuracy and final layout.

faq.question: Does it include print-readiness checks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes checklists and a Bash script for PDF dimensions, fonts, images, and file size.

faq.question: Does the package generate figures automatically?
faq.answer: No. It gives figure placement and caption guidance, but image generation is not included in this package.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it includes optional local shell commands for PDF inspection.

faq.question: What should I verify before printing?
faq.answer: Verify dimensions, fonts, image resolution, color contrast, QR codes, file size, and readability from distance.
